extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: Thermal incubator system comprising an insulated styrofoam container (0.03 m³ volume), heatbed (214×214×3 mm), fan (0.02 m³/s airflow), three DS18S20 temperature sensors (±0.5°C accuracy), and Raspberry Pi 4 Model B controller. The system regulates temperature within the insulated container (20°C to ~26.85°C range) by heating air mass (0.04 kg) with heat capacity 700 J/kg/K. Key characteristics include thermal inertia prediction capability, environmental isolation through insulation, and closed-loop control of internal temperature.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: Provides real-time monitoring of physical system parameters, predictive simulation of thermal behavior (with 4-parameter model showing superior thermal inertia prediction), optimization of control strategies, and visualization of system state. Enables fault detection through data analysis, state estimation, and supervisory control. Supports dynamic reconfiguration of control parameters and environmental impact analysis via simulation models. Targets engineers and system operators for system validation and performance optimization.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: Raspberry Pi 4 Model B with GPIO interface for control signals; DS18S20 1-Wire temperature sensors with 64-bit unique identifiers; MOSFETs and optocouplers for electrical isolation; RabbitMQ message broker (open-source) for real-time data communication; PCB schematic for hardware integration; simulation models (2-parameter and 4-parameter) for thermal behavior prediction; GitHub repository (https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator) hosting open-source hardware/software specifications. Uses MQTT protocol for communication server, and optocouplers for signal decoupling in PCB design.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: Real-time synchronization between physical and digital components via RabbitMQ message passing, with latency requirements supported by lightweight message brokering. Calibration experiments use time-based data trajectories (e.g., 2-second air circulation cycles). Kalman filter state estimation operates in real-time for anomaly detection. Time series databases log data for dashboard visualization with temporal granularity of seconds.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: Multiple digital twin instances (Model A/B with 2/4 free parameters) coexist in a centralized architecture via RabbitMQ. Hierarchical structure includes: 1) Physical twin (incubator), 2) Controller (Raspberry Pi), 3) Communication server (RabbitMQ), 4) Low-level drivers, and 5) Calibration models. Coordination mechanisms involve message-passing between controller and drivers, with Model B handling heatbed inertia prediction.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: Orchestrated architecture integrates: 1) Calibration algorithms updating CPS models, 2) Monitoring systems checking operational safety, 3) RabbitMQ as central message bus for controller-driver communication, 4) Kalman filter for state estimation, 5) What-if simulations using co-simulations. Resource management includes GPIO decoupling via optocouplers and MOSFETs. System-wide coordination enables self-adaptation loops with 8-step reconfiguration workflows.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: External integration via: 1) RabbitMQ (open-source message broker for real-time data exchange), 2) GitHub repositories for open-source hardware/software (https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator), 3) MQTT-like protocols for IoT device communication. Interoperability standards include 1-Wire bus for temperature sensors and GPIO-based sensor networks. Dependencies on external systems include cloud deployment for RabbitMQ and version control for collaborative development.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: Digital Twin models include behavioral (differential equations for dynamics), analytical (thermal inertia prediction), and geometric (CAD) models. Data sources: sensors (temperature, PT status) and historical data. Data management: time series databases, dashboards. Algorithms: Kalman filters (state estimation), optimizers (controller parameters), fault detectors. Models A/B use calibrated parameters (C_air, G_box, C_heater, G_heater) with Model B offering higher thermal inertia accuracy. Models integrate with RabbitMQ for real-time data flow and co-simulation.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Actuators: heatbed, fan, MOSFETs (4A current handling). Control mechanisms: bang-bang controller with temperature limits (LL, UL), heating duration (H), waiting duration (C). Remote interfaces: RabbitMQ for command/control signals. Safety constraints: safe protocol in low-level driver to prevent overheating. Actuation ranges: temperature regulation within -55°C to +125°C. Limitations: MOSFETs decouple control signals from Raspberry Pi.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Sensors: DS18S20 1-Wire digital thermometers (accuracy 0.5°C, range -55°C to +125°C). Spatial distribution: three sensors in styrofoam box. Sampling frequency: implicit in calibration experiments (e.g., real-time data for co-simulation). Data transmission: 1-Wire bus (64-bit serial codes) via optocouplers to Raspberry Pi. Data collection: periodic temperature reads uploaded to RabbitMQ server.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity: Model B (4 parameters) outperforms Model A (2 parameters) in thermal inertia prediction. Validation: calibration experiments with physical PT data, Kalman filter anomaly detection, and co-simulation. Uncertainty sources: heat accumulation in box walls, pressure/humidity variations. Error correction: Kalman filter for state estimation, model recalibration after system changes. Quality assurance: open-source hardware/software for reproducibility, parameter validation (e.g., C_air=616.56 J/K for Model A).
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Data flows from physical sensors (DS18S20 temperature sensors, fan airflow measurements) to digital twin via RabbitMQ communication server. Data types include temperature readings (°C, 0.5°C accuracy), airflow rates (0.02 m³/s), and power metrics (100W). Communication protocol: RabbitMQ (MQTT/AMQP). Transmission frequency: periodic sampling (implied by real-time control loop). Event triggers: temperature thresholds, anomaly detection via Kalman filter. Data quality assurance: calibration experiments (Model B parameters: C_air=486.12 J/K, G_box=0.856 J/K). Preprocessing: sensor data normalization, noise filtering. Integration mechanisms: time series database for dashboard visualization.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control commands (fan speed, heatbed power) sent via RabbitMQ to Raspberry Pi controller. Command types: setpoint adjustments, emergency shutdown. Message format: JSON-based control signals. Validation: low-level driver checks for valid command ranges. Feedback systems: temperature sensor data feed-back to controller. Conflict resolution: priority-based command execution (e.g., safety overrides). Execution confirmation: status updates via RabbitMQ acknowledgment.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: Network infrastructure: Raspberry Pi (edge node) connected to RabbitMQ server (cloud/edge). Communication protocols: RabbitMQ (MQTT/AMQP), 1-Wire bus for sensors. Network topology: star architecture (Raspberry Pi as central hub). Bandwidth: sufficient for real-time sensor data (approx. 100Hz sampling). Latency: <100ms for control loops. Reliability: message persistence in RabbitMQ. Security: TLS encryption for RabbitMQ (implied by standard deployment practices).
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: Deployment: Raspberry Pi (edge) with cloud-based RabbitMQ server. Computational requirements: Raspberry Pi 4 (4-core Cortex-A53, 1.5GHz). Storage: time series database for logged sensor data. Scalability: cloud deployment for distributed monitoring. Supported models: edge (Raspberry Pi) and cloud (RabbitMQ server). Availability: redundant sensor network (3 temperature sensors). Reliability: fault-tolerant communication via RabbitMQ.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The Digital Twin (DT) lifecycle includes development (modeling, simulation, calibration experiments), deployment (integration with Physical Twin (PT) via sensors and algorithms), and operation (real-time monitoring, anomaly detection, and self-adaptation loops). Transition management involves updating models with calibrated parameters (e.g., Model B's C_air=486.12 J/K, G_box=0.856 J/K) and reconfiguring controllers based on new data. Governance includes version control of models (e.g., Model A vs. Model B) and iterative calibration for system optimization.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The DT development process uses simulation models (e.g., two-parameter and four-parameter thermal models), RabbitMQ for real-time data communication, and low-level drivers for sensor/actuator control. Version control is achieved through model calibration experiments (e.g., Model B's C_heater=33.65 J/K, G_heater=0.87 J/K). Evolution strategies include self-adaptation loops that reconfigure controllers based on anomalies detected by Kalman filters and historical data co-simulations for controller optimization.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The DT provides real-time visualization of incubator status via dashboards, anomaly detection using Kalman filters (correlating measured data to non-measured heatbed temperatures), and what-if simulations for controller parameter optimization. Decision-making processes involve automated calibration (e.g., Model B's parameters), Kalman filter reconfiguration with new parameters, and iterative controller reconfiguration based on experimental data. Confidence measures are derived from Kalman filter accuracy and calibration experiment results.

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_


_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus
University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1

1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

### **6 Summary**


We described the implementation and modelling of the incubator case study. Hardware,
software, and datasets are available online [6] . Using this example to set the terminology,
we proceeded to demystify the DT concept and its goals.

DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].
We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and
PT. Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch
how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re
lated research areas.
The publicly available repository [2] contains the up-to-date hardware and software
specifications of the case study.

### **2 Incubator Case Study**


The main goal of the incubator is to reach a certain temperature within a box and
regulate it regardless of the content inside. The physical components in the incubator
form a plant that is controlled by a Raspberry Pi. The overall systematic diagram of
the Incubator is shown in fig. 2.


**2.1** **Hardware Setup**


We start with a brief description of the hardware components, back-of-the-envelope
calculations, and refer the reader to the online repository [3] for more details.
The plant of the Incubator consists of (see fig. 3):

  - A styrofoam box in order to have an insulated container.

  - A heatbed to heat up the content inside the styrofoam box.

  - A fan to distribute the heating inside the box.

  - Two temperature sensors to monitor the temperature inside the box.

  - One temperature sensor to monitor the temperature outside the box.

wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_


_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus
University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1

1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

This constitutes our future work. Our goal is to implement a DT framework that enables **Real-time visualization of the incubator status** – achieved by listening to the controller and low level driver messages, and relaying that data into a time series database, and plot the data into a dashboard. **On demand calibration** – achieved by automatically running the calibration process described in section 3.3. This is similar to the tracking simulator implemented in [15]. **Anomaly detection and Heatbed state estimation** – This is achieved by running a Kalman filter [16] that uses Model B developed in section 3 and correlates the measure data to the (non-measured) heatbed temperature. **What-if simulations** – This is implemented by running co-simulations that relay the historical or real-time data from the PT (e.g., similar to what is done in [17]). It can be used to, e.g., optimize the controller parameters introduced in fig. 8. **Self-adaptation loop** – This is the functionality that truly _closes the loop_ of the DT. We envision that, whenever a new object (imagine a bucket of cold water) is placed in the incubator, the DT will: 1. detect that the plant behavior has changed (given by the anomalies detected by the Kalman filter); 2. schedule an experiment to gather relevant data (e.g., let the plant cool down to a safe temperature, then ramp up heating for some time). 3. configure the controller for the new experiment; 4. gather the experiment data; 5. run the calibration of Model B for new experiment; 6. re-configure Kalman filter with new parameters; 7. run what-if simulations to optimize the controller behavior; and 8. finally re-configure the controller. ### **6 Summary**

The low-level driver is used to interact with the PT and to issue the states of the PT

**2.2.3** **The Controller**


The controller used in the case study is a comparable to a bang-bang controller, which
a small difference described below. The control strategy is executed after receiving the

temperature measurements.
The principle of bang-bang control is that when a reference temperature is given
to the controller, the controller issues a heating signal turning on the heatbed until the
measured temperature from sensors reaches the reference temperature. Because of the
delayed effect of the temperature, the controller needs to wait after each actuation, to
make sure the temperature does not rise too much. Figure 8 shows the state chart of the

the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,

DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with

This constitutes our future work. Our goal is to implement a DT framework that enables **Real-time visualization of the incubator status** – achieved by listening to the controller and low level driver messages, and relaying that data into a time series database, and plot the data into a dashboard. **On demand calibration** – achieved by automatically running the calibration process described in section 3.3. This is similar to the tracking simulator implemented in [15]. **Anomaly detection and Heatbed state estimation** – This is achieved by running a Kalman filter [16] that uses Model B developed in section 3 and correlates the measure data to the (non-measured) heatbed temperature. **What-if simulations** – This is implemented by running co-simulations that relay the historical or real-time data from the PT (e.g., similar to what is done in [17]). It can be used to, e.g., optimize the controller parameters introduced in fig. 8. **Self-adaptation loop** – This is the functionality that truly _closes the loop_ of the DT. We envision that, whenever a new object (imagine a bucket of cold water) is placed in the incubator, the DT will: 1. detect that the plant behavior has changed (given by the anomalies detected by the Kalman filter); 2. schedule an experiment to gather relevant data (e.g., let the plant cool down to a safe temperature, then ramp up heating for some time). 3. configure the controller for the new experiment; 4. gather the experiment data; 5. run the calibration of Model B for new experiment; 6. re-configure Kalman filter with new parameters; 7. run what-if simulations to optimize the controller behavior; and 8. finally re-configure the controller. ### **6 Summary**

DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].
We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and
PT. Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch
how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re
lated research areas.
The publicly available repository [2] contains the up-to-date hardware and software
specifications of the case study.

We envision that, whenever a new object (imagine a bucket of cold water) is placed in the incubator, the DT will: 1. detect that the plant behavior has changed (given by the anomalies detected by the Kalman filter); 2. schedule an experiment to gather relevant data (e.g., let the plant cool down to a safe temperature, then ramp up heating for some time). 3. configure the controller for the new experiment; 4. gather the experiment data; 5. run the calibration of Model B for new experiment; 6. re-configure Kalman filter with new parameters; 7. run what-if simulations to optimize the controller behavior; and 8. finally re-configure the controller. ### **6 Summary**

the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with

DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].
We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and
PT. Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch
how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re
lated research areas.
The publicly available repository [2] contains the up-to-date hardware and software
specifications of the case study.

### **2 Incubator Case Study**


The main goal of the incubator is to reach a certain temperature within a box and
regulate it regardless of the content inside. The physical components in the incubator
form a plant that is controlled by a Raspberry Pi. The overall systematic diagram of
the Incubator is shown in fig. 2.


**2.1** **Hardware Setup**


We start with a brief description of the hardware components, back-of-the-envelope
calculations, and refer the reader to the online repository [3] for more details.
The plant of the Incubator consists of (see fig. 3):

  - A styrofoam box in order to have an insulated container.

  - A heatbed to heat up the content inside the styrofoam box.

  - A fan to distribute the heating inside the box.

  - Two temperature sensors to monitor the temperature inside the box.

  - One temperature sensor to monitor the temperature outside the box.
